blurb: Multi-stem riparian tree with smooth grey bark and early catkins; fixes nitrogen.
seasons: Maroon-gold catkins early spring; grey to red-brown bark; short-lived
wildlife: Browse for deer/elk/rabbits; birds eat seeds; key insect host

care.hardiness: USDA zones 3–7 (rated hardy to zone 5 by most horticultural sources, but the thinleaf subspecies grows natively up to nearly 10,000 ft in Colorado and survives Front Range winters without any protection). A fast-growing but relatively short-lived tree — expect 30–50 years rather than centuries.
care.sun: Full sun to light part shade. In its native riparian canyon habitat it often grows in morning sun with afternoon dappled shade; in a garden setting it does fine in full sun as long as soil moisture is maintained.
care.soil: Requires consistently moist to wet, well-drained to moderately drained soil. Tolerates clay loam and even heavy clay well. Adapts to both mildly acidic and mildly alkaline pH — Front Range soil chemistry is not a barrier, but dry, lean upland soils should be avoided. A nitrogen-fixing symbiont (Frankia) in its roots makes it a soil-builder rather than a nitrogen consumer — no amendment is needed for fertility.
care.water: High — this is a riparian and streamside tree that performs best with reliable moisture. Site it in a low spot, near a water feature, on a rain-garden berm, or in any area that stays moist (seasonal flooding is tolerated). Without steady moisture it declines; it is not a candidate for dry xeric sites. Deep weekly irrigation supports it where natural moisture is limited.
care.spacing: Allow 12–20 ft between stems (or from other structures) at planting — mature trees reach 15–30 ft tall and 12–20 ft wide, and the rhizomatous root system pushes outward. When used as a thicket for erosion control or wildlife habitat, closer spacing (8–10 ft) is fine and the colony fills in naturally.
care.bloom: One of the earliest flowering woody plants on the Front Range: maroon-gold catkins (male) and small upright female catkins appear on bare branches in March–April, before any leaf unfurls. Small woody 'cones' (strobiles) develop through summer and persist into winter — a quiet winter interest feature.
care.feeding: None needed. The root-associated nitrogen-fixing bacteria (Frankia) supply the tree's nitrogen requirements and improve surrounding soil. Fertilizing is unnecessary and may encourage overly lush, disease-prone growth.
care.maintenance: Very little required. Remove any dead or crossing branches in late winter. Manage suckers and root-sprouts if you want to keep a defined multi-stem form rather than a spreading thicket. When grown as a thicket for habitat or bank stabilization, leave the suckers to colonize freely. Old stems on a thicket can be thinned or coppiced to the ground in late winter to encourage fresh vigorous growth — new stems emerge quickly.
care.selfsow: Spreads actively by rhizomes and root suckers to form dense multi-stem stands. In a naturalistic riparian planting this is a virtue; in a smaller garden keep it in check by severing suckers at their base with a sharp spade each spring.
care.troubles: Generally robust and pest-free in Colorado riparian settings. Woolly alder aphids occasionally cluster on stems; tent caterpillars may visit. Leaf rust and occasional leaf spot are minor. The main threat is drought — a tree sited on a dry upland without supplemental water will decline and die. Not browsed as heavily as willows but deer and elk do eat young stems in winter.
care.companions: Ideal with other native riparian and moisture-tolerant species: chokecherry, red-twig dogwood, Wood's rose, native willows (*Salix* spp.), golden currant, wild iris (*Iris missouriensis*). Its nitrogen-fixing ability particularly benefits the surrounding shrub layer. A natural colony planting along a dry creek bed or bioswale is the most ecologically productive use.
care.planting: Plant in early spring while still dormant, or in early fall (September) before freeze-up — both seasons let the root system establish in cool, moist soil. Prepare a large planting hole and keep the site consistently moist throughout the first growing season; without reliable water the young tree will stall. Containerized nursery stock (1–5 gal) is the typical source since seed availability is limited. Mountain alder is not a good long-term container subject — its high moisture needs and eventual size (15–30 ft) mean it should go into the ground promptly; a pot can work for a season of establishment, but must be watered far more frequently than in-ground stock and must not be allowed to dry out even briefly. Sink or heel-in the pot over winter if it cannot be planted out before freeze.
care.propagation: Seed is the primary natural propagation route but requires attention: collect strobiles (cones) in late summer before they fully dry and release seed, air-dry briefly, and clean. Seeds from some lots germinate readily without treatment; others benefit from 4 weeks of cold-moist stratification at 34–40°F, especially dried seed. Sow shallowly in a moist seedbed in full light — germination requires light and temperatures of 50–77°F. Viability is generally low and seeds should not be stored beyond 1–2 years. Vegetatively, the easiest method is dividing rooted root-crown sprouts or suckers from an established clump with a spade in early spring. Hardwood cuttings taken in winter have also been used successfully. For streambank or bioswale restoration, bare-root or container whips from a native plant nursery are the most reliable start.
